sentences.text: OK.
When I first came to this first city what they put us together to send us to Germany, I looked around and I picked a girl from my town.
She was a little older, very quiet and very-- and good disposition, you know, and a reputation.
So I told her I would like to be your sister.
I would like to be, whatever happens to us, that we have each other.
I was smart enough this time to do this.
And we were always together, sharing our little [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, cupboards, everything, put together the food.
And we were together until-- I'll tell you the story what happened to us, given I'm there.
Right now, we are just walking out in-- from this-- from our Grunberg.

And we were walking, and on the way, they took us to Helmbrechts, this is a [? church. ?] Well, they always put us mountains and lakes, whatever they thought maybe the Russians or the Americans could meet us, they were trying to avoid.
So they were taking all this long distances for us.
So we were walking.
Her name was Tzipora Margakevitch.
She's supposed to be in Haifa in Israel.
So we were walking, and it was-- I-- I just say that everybody should have a faith, but special we had such a faith.
We knew that we we're going to survive with this condition, but still we-- we were thinking about walking, and walking, and walking, beside our pains and everything.
So we were walking in a little town, Helmbrechts.
They put us in a room, and there was no facilities for bathroom, for nothing.
They put in the front a-- a pail, and that's-- and took us all naked.
And that's how we were sitting there in Helmbrechts.
I'm going to go there now.
That was in Helmbrechts?
Helmbrechts.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah.
We were young.
They put us in a-- in a-- a big pail of water.
That's supposed to be the bath, and that's naked, how we-- we were sitting there.
No food, nothing.
And that was the worst--
Yeah, the worst spot in our-- in our experience from this.
But then they took us out and they send us further, further, further.
No.
I don't-- I know we had just rags around, yeah.
Yeah.
So-- and I was 35 kilo, which is 70 pounds.
And that's how-- how it went, till when we came to Czechoslovakia.
Something unusual happened.
I'm missing a lot, because I'm going back.
OK.
On the way, was-- was impossible, impossible.
One time, I had a big pain here, and it was like pus.
And this was my biggest this.
And it needs a little [? ichthammol ?] ointment.
It needs something to bite off.
Well, it was such a painful-- and I couldn't complain, because that's the only thing during a march like this.
But I remember that one of our girls was a nurse in-- in the camp.
So I approached her.
My mind was working.
And I said I'll give you a piece of beets.
I had here found a piece of beets.
Give me a drop of your [? ichthammol. ?] And she had a drop of this ointment.
So I put it down and busted, and I was relieved.
And she had the beets.
That was a very remembering experience from the march.
Yeah.
And what else happened?
Many things laying in there under the rain, in the woods or in the streets.
It was terrible.
And no staple.
Yeah.
But they always had where to go, and they did not-- was not with us.
No food.
No food.
It was no question about food, because they took us to-- to die out and to be free.
They didn't take us to survive.
Yes Yes.
Each time when we walk out from the stable was a mountain of-- of-- of bones from our girls what died, or they shot, whatever.
And one time, I wanted to be there.
I really wanted.
So I-- that was by the end.
I lay with these girls, and when-- when was quiet down, I felt I'm still alive, and walked out.
I was many times on the mountain of the dead girls, the skeletons.
I was in a very bad-- Grunberg was very bad for women.
It was one of the worse belonging from-- to Auschwitz, the worst of Auschwitz.
So I don't know where I am standing right now.
On the march.
OK.
Yeah.
I walked out, and walk.
So we came to a city, Carlsbad, which was beautiful, the sunshine, and people on balconies.
And we didn't-- we couldn't think.
Are we the crazy people, or are they are the crazy people?
That was on my mind.
I couldn't know who's crazy here.
Here is such a luxury, so beautiful.
And we are in-- in dirty towels, whatever we had on us.
I cannot remember how we was clothed.
No.
This is in Czechoslovakia.
I'm going there now.
I'm going there now.
So in-- in Marienbad, [NON-ENGLISH], this is the most beautiful places we had to pass by in this condition.
So we approached--
I'm approaching now this.
We approached a little town, Tos.
Tos was the town in Czechoslovakia.
And the first time we saw that people were throwing out, like, pieces of sugar, like this thick piece of sugar.
So one fell on the other.
The girls starting almost to fight, who is going to grab the piece of sugar.
And that was the-- the-- the town what I never forgot, and I'm going to there to see this town, Tos in Czechoslovakia.
But in Germany, we didn't have one lady or one man to-- to throw something when we were walking by.
It's amazing.
Maybe they were afraid.
That was my feelings.
No, that was like the homeless here.
A lot of people go by.
They want to help.
And I'm walking by the homeless here.
I never see that a person should walk to the homeless and give something.
That's terrible.
And I did once.
And she looked at me.
I said, I have silver dollars, and I want you to have it.
And she said, go away, I don't need money.
I don't want it.
I forced her.
I gave her those two silver dollars.
That's-- that's how it is.
The same feelings was-- they were-- they were looking at us.
They didn't know what it is.
Like a-- like a circus, like crazy people, or whatever.
Nobody--
I don't know.
I cannot answer this because I don't know.
We were just a number, which is to destroy.
That was our name, destroy number.
No.
I-- I didn't have a number.
No.
So I'm approaching now to the end of this march, which was five months.
And it was like--
Yes.
I marched five months.
But a lot of girls were smart and very aggressive.
I was not aggressive.
They ran away.
They took a chance, and-- and they were all right.
They were working for the Germans, or they somehow survived.
In the beginning of this, and they did.
But I-- I walk, a good soldier until the end of the-- of the story.
Till the liberation.
I walked until the 5th of May.
I-- I stayed for four years in the camp.
And I have here, I-- I was walking.
Yeah.
From January 1st till May 5th.
How many survived?
Roughly, I can say maybe-- maybe 70.
And right now, I'm going to know the number when I get to-- to Prague.
We were divided.
I had-- went to Bergen-Belsen, and I went toward Czechoslovakia.
In our-- I don't know.
But I don't know how many survived.
I will know.
I have the list, but we don't have the time.
I have a list.
Yeah.
What happened is it was maybe the beginning of May, and we were very, very sick.
The beginning of May.
That was '45.
And that was-- and it was just like, let's say 20 girls left.
So they decide, the SS people, they decide to-- to take us on a truck into the woods and shoot us.
And we waited for this.
We waited.
It was nothing that we don't know.
That's where we're going.
And on the way, a miracle happened.
The Americans bombed our truck.
And next to me was sitting this SS woman.
I don't know if she was pregnant with this SS man or not.
But she was sitting right next to me.
And a piece of bomb went into her stomach, to her legs, something.
And didn't touch my hand, nothing.
And I said to myself, I think now is the time.
I have to act.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said-- and I pushed my friend Tzipora Margakevitch.
I said I want to jump down from this, because we are going to be shot anyway.
I'll take a chance.
Now they are busy with themselves.
The SS men, they're looking for her wounds.
I want to.
She said she's not going.
So I pulled her down by force.
And there was one blanket, and we both was covered with our blanket, and we wanted to run away.
It was a big water.
So we couldn't swim.
I didn't know how to swim.
But we took a chance.
The water was covering-- if life is [INAUDIBLE]-- covering till our necks.
We went by this water, and we came to woods.
It was woods, trees with snow, still, in May there.
So we laid down.
And then-- and we was-- So what's now?
What are we going to do now?
So we saw from far away a little dock house, or whatever.
We went there.
We stealed the-- the plate with the old potatoes, whatever it was, their food.
And we had the typhus, you know, from this cold thing.
And we were sitting there, and we were listening, listening.
And then I said, now it's time.
We have to walk out and see what's to do.
So when we walk out, a German man passed by.
And he said, you know what?
There was shot 18 girls.
They-- the rest of them, they all shot them.
From the truck?
From this truck.
And-- and sure enough, we-- and we sit there, and we walked, walked, and we saw a stable.
And I went into the stable with this girl.
With Tzipora.
We covered ourself under the straw, first of all.
And we decide when the owner will come in, then we are going to move, and she will scream, and we tell her.
So that's what happened.
And she said, Jesus Christ!
And she screams.
And we moved without the straw.
And she said, what is this?
And we told her.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], don't be afraid.
We are just this and this, and we would like to stay here.
What did you tell her you were?
You were--
Jewish.
Yeah.
She said, don't stay here, because the German Wehrmacht comes here for straw, for things, and they're going to shoot you.
I said, don't worry, and what should we do?
So she said, go up to a little mountain there, sit there, and I'll come there.
So she came, and we beg her for a towel or a little water to wash up.
Because our skin was this black, and it was filled with lice.
The whole skin was with with lice.
[NON-ENGLISH] .
Yeah.
So we just needed water more than-- than anything.
So she brought the water, and then she decided, you know-- what happened?
Then the French prisoners were there, all the French prisoners.
But they have more freedom, and they were different treated.
Yeah No, they were passing by.
And they asked us, qu'est-ce que c'est?
What is this?
So what could we say?
We couldn't speak French.
And we-- we didn't have to say much.
You look at us, and you knew.
So they said to wait here.
They run out.
They came back, they brought sardines, all kind of things what we shouldn't even look at that.
But they-- they went to this woman, and they said, definite, she has to let us in in the stable.
So we went back in and we washed up.
And then after a few days, they-- they came with a white flag, and they told us the war is over.
And what was the date of that?
That was like the 4th of May.
OK.
Now.
The French-- the French decided that we should go with the Red Cross.
We should go.
But then the American soldiers came into this town.
I don't know how.
And they built from a little school in Volary, which I'm going to this building, which is a factory now.
They made a little hospital.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Volary is near Prague, but we came out to this little town.
Volary.
So we were there, and we were treated like babies, with a little bottle of farina.
And we were sweating for two weeks.
Every day has to be three times changed.
We were sweating from weakness.
American.
Yeah.
It's very nice to tell you about that, that In the same little hospital that was made was the SS woman in a room, healed for her wounds from the bomb.
And we had to face this, too.
That happened.
I don't know why.
I-- I was so sick and so tired, that I couldn't even think about why she was there.
I know she was there.
And beside the point, they were carrying me down, a little down, because my heart was enlarged, and two fingers.
They didn't let me walk, from the walking, from the hunger.
So I was carried up and down, up and down in this-- in this little-- it was no elevators.
It was stairs.
And after a while, the American soldiers had a little speech to us, a little talk, that they-- they think we should go to Israel.
In the meantime, they took us to Salzburg.
Yeah.
They took us to Salzburg, which is Austria, into a camp, in the meantime.
Yes.
There was nothing to eat anyway.
But we didn't need much to eat, and we had probably already.
It was Salzburg.
Yeah.
Just no name.
And while I was there, there was many people gathering in the same camp.
And one man approached me, which happened to be my husband's best friend.
And he was with him, in [INAUDIBLE], in that camp.
And he said, I know you.
Are you Adela Kestenberg?
I said yes.
He said you groom-- I was engaged.
I was six--
I was engaged, yeah.
And-- but a week after my engagement I was taken to camp.
Six years.
Yeah, six years I was separated.
I was a bride six years.
And he was in different problems, Mauthausen, the worst thing, and I was in different.
So he said don't you get in touch with nobody, no nothing, because he is in Paris.
My fiance's in Paris.
And then-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I didn't take this for-- for very serious, because I didn't plan to-- to get anything.
I was really sick and tired, and nobody in the world.
No brothers.
No hope for parents.
Nobody.
So I said OK.
Yeah, I knew everything.
We knew.
Tzipora?
Tzipora was, I think, liberated the same time as me.
And we came-- afterwards, we-- she decided to go to Israel, because she had a brother there in kibbutz from before the war.
But when-- when we wanted to go, and she was hanging outside clothes, and the Red Cross left.
So that was meant to be not to go.
Everything is meant to be in life.
So we didn't go.
And we went to another camp, Foehrenwald.
Same what Solomon.
Yeah.
Maybe different.
Foehrenwald, [NON-ENGLISH].
This is also in Germany.
Yeah.
It's close to Munich.
So I was there.
And while I was there, I got a letter.
I had a book, who was alive.
So I read that Avram Manheimer, a brother of my fiance, is alive.
And he is in the Feldafing camp.
So he came, and he said I should stay with his wife.
He already-- he was 20 years old in '50, when he went to Auschwitz and Warsaw ghetto.
And he said I should wait there with his wife, and he will go to Paris to see his brother.
And he picked him up.
While he picked him up, he was already very sick, because he ran away from a running train.
And they shoot after him in the ribs, and he was very sick.
The German--
Yeah.
The German people told him-- he went in in a house, and very friendly, and he thought that's it, that he is safe.
But they went and they called the police, and the police took him to Mauthausen of [INAUDIBLE] kommander for punishment.
And he had very bad working under the ground in [NON-ENGLISH], and was very bad in making airplanes.
And he was very sick.
So the bottom line is, when I came out, I found him very sick.
So I happened to be--
45 years his nurse.
To Feldafing.
Where his brother was.
But he was not well, because from all this working a few years under the ground with no air, with no food.
He was wounded, yeah.
How can he be well enough?
So he had to go to a sanatorium right away.
That was in the chest.
Yeah, in-- in Gauting.
I got married in '47, March. 30Th of March, '47.
And from this day I was behind him.
He told me that I am not obligated.
I was just engaged, and wife leaves the husband in this condition.
He-- he want me to be free and have a happy life, because I was happy and lucky to come out OK.
But I said, Naomi and Ruth.
Whatever will happen to you, will happen to me.
You are not so lucky, but you're lucky that I will take care.
And I took care 45 years.
After that time, he was better.
And after we got married-- that was '48, '45-- three years later, I had my son.
And this was the biggest blessing.
In Germany, yeah.
Born in Feldafing, in the camp.
I have one son and one daughter.
My daughter was born in Cleveland, Ohio.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'll talk about them later.
I still want to talk about my husband.
He was a very great guy, very nice, very good family.
And we had a good relation.
Besides, he was a good patient.
Never-- never aggravated, and never-- no nothing.
Everything was good.
Everything was good.
He was very happy that he had somebody to help him, and devoted like me.
And the children were good, and everything was good.
But he was--
